text: This video demonstrates technical caveats and surgical modifications during robotic radical prostatectomy in a renal transplant recipient. Performing radical prostatectomy in a kidney transplant recipient could be more challenging due to presence of graft in iliac fossa. Our patient was sixty one year old with good performance status with history of live kidney transplant in the right iliac fossa in two thousand thirteen. with present creatinine levels of three. His screening PSA was fifteen point three and on DRE right prostate lobe was formed. Multiparametric MRI revealed PIRADS IV lesion in the right lobe with normal SV. Trust biopsy revealed GLSEN IV plus IV cancer. He was counselled regarding the curative options of robotic radical prostatectomy or radiotherapy and he opted for surgery.

Preoperative preparation included nephrology opinion regarding change in immunosuppression and fitness for surgery. non-contrast MRI for detailed anatomy of transplant kidney and ureter and local staging of prostate cancer. We planned few modifications to our surgical approach as surface marking of raft kidney to plan port placement, initial diagnostic laparoscopy via a five mm palmar's point, right-sided twelve mm assistant port was placed in superior medial location to safeguard transplant kidney. Retropubic space was developed largely from contralateral side to avoid injury to the transplant kidney and ureter. Pelvic lymph anatomy on the side of the graft was avoided. Standard RARP position was made.

The surgical team observes no intraperitoneal adhesions, with the transplant kidney securely positioned in the retroperitoneal space. Ports are placed under direct vision to protect the graft, and the robotic system is docked. The procedure begins with releasing adhesions of the sigmoid colon. Instruments are inserted under visual guidance to avoid graft injury. Posterior dissection proceeds in a standard manner, identifying bilateral vas deferens and dissecting seminal vesicles to the posterior bladder neck. The bladder drop starts from the left side to prevent damage to the transplant kidney and ureter. The peritoneum is incised lateral to the medial umbilical ligament, developing the retropubic space. The right-sided dissection is performed cautiously to protect the transplant ureter, with denser perivesical fat slowing the process and minimal bleeding observed.

The surgeon performs dissection lateral to the prostate, carefully preserving periureteric soft tissue near the transplant ureter. The bilateral endopelvic fascia are exposed and incised, followed by division of the anterior bladder neck. The catheter is delivered, and the posterior bladder neck division is completed. Bilateral vas deferens and seminal vesicles are delivered, and the lateral pedicles are clipped and divided.

The surgeon completes the remaining steps of the procedure in a standard fashion, bags the specimen, and performs left-sided extended pelvic lymph node dissection. Right-sided pelvic lymph node dissection is avoided to prevent injury to the graft vessels and ureter. A two-layer vesicourethral anastomosis is completed.

Total console time was one thirty two minutes. Operative time was one sixty two minutes. Estimated blood loss was hundred ml. Perioperative clinical course was uneventful. Renal functions remained stable with creatinine at discharge of two point nine. Final histopathology was PT two C four plus three adenocarcinoma with unifocal positive margins at right apex. all lymph nodes were negative for malignancy at three months psa is zero point zero one and he is using two to three diapers per day to conclude robotic radical prostate mean renal transplant recipient is feasible and safe few modifications to standard technique are required in these patients to achieve comparable outcomes thank you
